mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable LLM serving, leveraging advanced techniques like PagedAttention and continuous batching. Key trade-offs include the need for GPU resources for optimal performance and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the vLLM Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official vLLM Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install vLLM easily.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.18.0

helm repo update

```

> This adds the vLLM repository for version 0.18.0.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install vLLM with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs vLLM in the specified namespace, ensuring that all necessary resources are created.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.18.0

```

> Installs vLLM in the 'vllm' namespace.

steps.title: Create a vLLM Custom Resource (CR)
steps.description:
This step creates a Custom Resource that defines the configuration for your LLM model serving.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: my-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

```

> Replace 'gpt-3' with your desired model name.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: my-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

steps.title: Verify the vLLM deployment
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the vLLM deployment and ensures that the pods are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n vllm

```

> You should see the vLLM pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step removes the vLLM deployment and associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all related data and configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command removes the vLLM release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data. This step will upgrade your vLLM installation to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.18.0

kubectl uncordon <node-name>

kubectl get pods -n vllm

```

> Replace <node-name> with the name of the node you want to drain.
> This command upgrades vLLM to version 0.18.0.
> Replace <node-name> with the name of the node you drained earlier.
> Verify that the upgraded pods are running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified container image may not exist or is inaccessible.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and ensure it is available in the specified registry.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM pod is failing to start due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n vllm' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster does not have enough CPU resources to schedule the vLLM pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the available CPU resources in your cluster or reduce the resource requests in the vLLM configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Context deadline exceeded'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The request to the vLLM service is timing out, possibly due to high load or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check the service configuration and ensure that the vLLM pods are healthy and responsive.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new models and improved memory management features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the vLLM pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n vllm' showing status 'Running'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
